key_findings: CD8+ T cells are annotated as a fine-grained fetal immune cell type in prenatal skin (7-17 PCW).
T cells (including CD8+) emerge later in gestation, accompanying thymus, bone marrow and spleen formation from around 10 PCW.
Innate immune cells (macrophages, ILCs) precede adaptive immune cells (B cells, T cells) in prenatal skin.
Single-cell alpha-beta TCR sequencing was performed to resolve T cell subsets.
Pre-dermal condensate cells co-locate with dendritic and lymphoid cells, suggesting immune cell participation in early hair follicle formation niches.
Prenatal skin matrix cells express CXCL14, a chemokine that recruits Treg cells, highlighting potential immune-HF interactions.
CyTOF and flow cytometry characterization of immune cells in human fetal skin (second trimester).
CD8+ T cells in fetal skin are predominantly naive with high Ki-67, CD27, CD31 and low CD25, CD45RO.
Up to 30% of CD8+ T cells in fetal skin are CD45RO+ (memory-like phenotype).
CD45RO+ fetal CD8+ T cells show higher Nur77 levels, suggesting antigen-dependent TCR signaling.
Fetal skin CD8+ T cells produce substantially less TNF-alpha than adult skin counterparts.
IFN-gamma production by fetal CD8+ T cells does not statistically differ from adults when populations are evaluated as a whole.
On a per-cell basis (normalized to CD45RO), fetal CD45RO+ CD8+ T cells show enriched IFN-gamma production.
CD31 expression is elevated on fetal skin T cells, suggesting enrichment for recent thymic emigrants.
The proportion of CD45RO+ cells in fetal CD8+ T cells remains relatively constant across second trimester (unlike Tregs which progressively increase).
Cross-organ developing human immune system atlas spanning 7-17 PCW.
T cell progenitors restricted to thymus, reflecting stringent niche requirements for T cell development.
Conventional T cells (CD4+, CD8+, Tregs) identified across prenatal organs including skin.
Adult naive and central memory T cells had prenatal counterparts, but resident/effector memory T cells did not.
Broad shift from innate to adaptive immune populations across gestation.
Conserved processes of proliferation and maturation for T cells prior to migration from thymus to peripheral tissues.
Memory T cells may appear after 17 PCW.
Identified a unique population of TCR alpha-beta-gamma-delta+ (DP) T cells in fetal human skin.
DP alpha-beta-gamma-delta T cells are CD4+CD8+ in early second trimester, trending toward single-positive CD4+ and CD8+ later.
DP alpha-beta-gamma-delta T cells express markers of naive cells and recent thymic emigrants (CD45RA, CD31).
SP alpha-beta T cells show stronger interactions with skin cell populations compared to DP T cells.
Fetal skin T cells were found exclusively in the dermis in situ.
Only alpha-beta TCR-expressing T cells confirmed in fetal skin (no gamma-delta-only T cells detected by earlier studies).
Review of the developing immune network in prenatal skin.
Fetal skin contains both CD4+ and CD8+ T cells; naive CD4+ T cells are the most abundant and increase across development.
Fetal skin contains 4-7% the abundance of T cells found in adult skin.
Fetal skin T cells are predominantly naive, with very few memory-effector/CLA+ T cells even into the third trimester.
Lower CCL27 expression in fetal skin may partly explain reduced T cell homing.
Tregs constitute 10-20% of total T cell pool in fetal skin, comparable to adult.
Seeding of naive T cells in fetal skin occurs independently of CCL27.
Epidermal T cells have not been identified in fetal skin; T cells reside in the dermis.
Phenotypic characterization of leukocytes in prenatal human dermis.
Majority of fetal skin CD3+ cells express CD4; CD8+ T cells are a minor population.
Only alpha-beta TCR-expressing CD3+ cells observed in fetal skin.
Fetal skin T cell homing may occur independently of CCL27.
Fetal intestinal CD8+ T cells with memory phenotype detected, analogous to fetal skin findings.
Functional cytotoxic capacity (cytokine and granzyme B production) of fetal/infant CD8+ T cells markedly reduced compared to adult.
High PD-1 expression on fetal/infant effector CD8+ T cells.
Impaired CD8+ T cell functionality contributes to tolerance during foreign antigen exposure.
Review of tissue-resident immune populations in the fetus.
Adaptive fetal immune memory observed after gestational week 14, following thymic emigration.
Fetal environment prioritizes immune tolerance and preferential differentiation of naive T cells into Tregs.
Fetal CD8+ T cell immunity is functional in antigen recognition, even if less potent (decreased cytotoxicity) compared to adult.
Evidence for establishing resident CD4 and CD8 memory (with unknown specificity) in gut and skin in utero.
Innate-like features of CD4+ and CD8+ T cells retained from fetal period.
